meta.title: The First Three Minutes of a Transformer
meta.subtitle: Everything that happens to raw text before Layer 1 — a Weinbergian chronicle of GPT-2's preprocessing pipeline
meta.description: A deep dive into everything that happens to raw text BEFORE it enters Layer 1 of GPT-2 — tokenization, embeddings, positional encoding, and the birth of the residual stream.
meta.author: Peter Balogh

children.children: In 1977, the physicist Steven Weinberg wrote a book called "The First Three Minutes," chronicling everything that happened in the universe between the Big Bang and t = 180 seconds. His argument was simple: those first moments determined everything that followed — the ratio of hydrogen to helium, the seeds of galaxies, the shape of the cosmos. But nobody could see them directly. You had to reconstruct them from theory and inference.

Transformers have their own first three minutes. When you type a sentence into a language model, a remarkable sequence of transformations happens before the neural network's first layer even fires. Raw text — mere squiggles on a screen — gets converted into high-dimensional geometry. Characters become tokens. Tokens become vectors. Vectors get stamped with positional information. And then, and only then, does the transformer begin its work.

This tutorial follows that invisible pipeline in one specific, concrete model: GPT-2 (small), released by OpenAI in February 2019. We'll use its real numbers, its actual architecture, its specific design choices. Not a generic transformer — this one, with its 50,257-token vocabulary, its 768-dimensional embeddings, and its 1,024-position context window.

Everything in this tutorial happens BEFORE GPT-2's first transformer layer. No attention. No feed-forward networks. Just tokenization, two table lookups, and an addition. And yet, by the end, we'll have converted a string of characters into a rich geometric object that encodes both meaning and position.

props.title: Act I: From Characters to Tokens

children.children: It starts with nothing more than a string. When you type "The cat sat on the mat" into GPT-2, what arrives at the system boundary is a sequence of Unicode characters:

That's it. No structure. No words. No meaning. Just bytes. The computer doesn't know where one word ends and another begins. It doesn't know that "the" appears twice. It doesn't know that "cat" is a noun or that "sat" is past tense. It sees a flat sequence of numbers.

Imagine receiving a telegram written in a language you don't speak. You can see the individual symbols, count them, compare them — but you have no idea what they mean. That's where GPT-2 starts. Every bit of "understanding" it eventually displays is built up from this starting point of pure ignorance.
The first task is to carve this flat stream of characters into meaningful chunks. Not words exactly — something both more principled and more strange. We need tokens.

How do you decide what the "meaningful chunks" of a language are? You could use words — split on spaces and punctuation. But this has problems. What about "don't"? Is that one word or two? What about "running" — should the model see it as one thing, or recognize that it shares a root with "run"? And what happens when the model encounters "Pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanoconiosis"?

GPT-2 uses a technique called Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE), and understanding it is essential to understanding everything that follows. BPE doesn't use linguistic rules about words or morphemes. It uses statistics. Pure, beautiful statistics.

GPT-2's tokenizer was trained on WebText — a dataset of about 8 million web pages (roughly 40 GB of text) scraped from outbound links on Reddit that received at least 3 upvotes. This is the corpus from which the tokenizer learned what chunks of text are worth treating as units.
The Training Phase: Building the Merge Table
BPE starts from the most atomic possible representation and iteratively builds up larger units:
Suppose our entire training corpus consists of these words (with frequencies):

low     (5 times)
lower   (2 times)
newest  (6 times)
widest  (3 times)
Step 1: Start by splitting every word into individual characters, with a special end-of-word marker ⟨/w⟩:

l o w ⟨/w⟩       × 5
l o w e r ⟨/w⟩   × 2
n e w e s t ⟨/w⟩ × 6
w i d e s t ⟨/w⟩ × 3
Step 2: Count all adjacent pairs across the corpus:
Step 3: Merge the most frequent pair (e, s) → es everywhere:

l o w ⟨/w⟩         × 5
l o w e r ⟨/w⟩     × 2
n e w es t ⟨/w⟩    × 6
w i d es t ⟨/w⟩    × 3
Step 4: Recount pairs. Now (es, t) appears 9 times — merge it into est:

l o w ⟨/w⟩         × 5
l o w e r ⟨/w⟩     × 2
n e w est ⟨/w⟩     × 6
w i d est ⟨/w⟩     × 3

And so on. Each merge creates a new "token" — a frequently occurring character sequence. After thousands of merges, you end up with a vocabulary of common subwords. GPT-2's tokenizer performed exactly this process on WebText until it accumulated 50,257 tokens.

BPE is like a data compression algorithm that discovers the repeating patterns in a language. Just as ZIP files find repeated byte sequences and replace them with shorter codes, BPE finds repeated character sequences and promotes them to first-class tokens. The difference is that BPE's "compression dictionary" becomes the model's vocabulary — the set of atomic units it thinks in.

props.title: Why BPE Converges on Something Useful

children.children: It might seem surprising that a purely statistical procedure — just counting and merging — would discover linguistically meaningful units. But it does, and here's why:
Common words like "the", "and", "is" appear so frequently that they quickly become single tokens.
Common morphemes like "-ing", "-tion", "-ment", "un-" also appear frequently and get merged into single tokens.
The process naturally creates a hierarchy: characters → common pairs → common subwords → common words. Rare words stay decomposed into common subwords.
This means the model never encounters a truly "unknown" word. Even "Pneumonoultramicroscopicsilicovolcanoconiosis" gets broken into recognizable pieces.

GPT-2 uses a specific variant called byte-level BPE. Instead of starting with Unicode characters (of which there are ~150,000), it starts with raw bytes (of which there are exactly 256). Every possible text — English, Chinese, emoji, binary — can be represented as a sequence of bytes.

This means GPT-2's base vocabulary is 256 byte tokens, and the remaining ~50,000 entries in its vocabulary are merges built on top of those bytes. The advantage: the tokenizer can handle any input, in any language, without any preprocessing. The disadvantage: non-English text and rare symbols often get split into many byte-level tokens, making processing less efficient.

GPT-2 also applies a regex-based pre-tokenization step that splits text on word boundaries before BPE. This prevents merges from crossing word boundaries — the tokenizer won't merge the 'ed' at the end of 'walked' with the space and 'i' at the start of 'into'. The regex pattern looks roughly like this:

# GPT-2's pre-tokenization pattern (simplified):
pat = r"""'s|'t|'re|'ve|'m|'ll|'d| ?\w+| ?\d+| ?[^\s\w\d]+|\s+"""

BPE produces a fixed merge table — an ordered list of 50,000+ character pair merges. This table, combined with the 256 base byte tokens, defines GPT-2's vocabulary of exactly 50,257 entries. It was determined once during tokenizer training on WebText and never changes.
Now let's watch GPT-2's trained tokenizer process actual text. The tokenizer has its merge table memorized and applies the merges greedily, from highest-priority to lowest.

props.title: Tokenizing 'The cat sat on the mat'

children.children: Using GPT-2's actual tokenizer:

Input:  "The cat sat on the mat"

Tokens: ["The", " cat", " sat", " on", " the", " mat"]

Token IDs: [464, 3797, 3332, 319, 262, 2291]
Notice several things:
"The" (capitalized, start of sentence) is token 464.
" the" (lowercase, with leading space) is a DIFFERENT token: 262. GPT-2's tokenizer treats leading spaces as part of the token.
Each common English word is a single token. These are frequent enough that BPE merged all their characters during training on WebText.
The output is a sequence of integers. From this point on, the original characters are gone. GPT-2 works entirely with these IDs.

props.title: What About Rare and Complex Words?

children.children: The real magic of BPE shows when we look at less common words:

"sitting"     → [" sitting"]             → [5765]            (1 token — very common)
"sat"          → [" sat"]                 → [3332]            (1 token — common)
"concatenate" → [" conc", "aten", "ate"] → [1673, 3371, 378] (3 tokens)
"tokenizer"   → [" token", "izer"]       → [11241, 7509]     (2 tokens)
"BPE"          → ["B", "PE"]              → [33, 11401]       (2 tokens)

See the pattern? Common words are single tokens. Less common words get split into common subwords. Very rare words get split into many pieces. This is the compression at work — the tokenizer allocates its limited vocabulary budget of 50,257 entries to the patterns it saw most often in WebText.

Think of the tokenizer as a mosaic artist with a fixed set of 50,257 tiles. Common shapes (sky blue, forest green) come in large pieces. But when the artist needs an unusual color — say, a specific shade of magenta — they assemble it from smaller tiles. No image is unrepresentable; some just require more tiles than others.

GPT-2's tokenizer encodes spaces by attaching them to the beginning of the following token. A leading space is represented as the Unicode character 'Ġ' (a G with a dot above it) in the vocabulary file. So the vocabulary actually contains entries like:

Token 262:  "Ġthe"   → decodes as " the"  (with leading space)
Token 464:  "The"    → decodes as "The"   (no space — sentence start)
Token 1169: "Ġcat"   → decodes as " cat"
Token 9246: "cat"    → decodes as "cat"   (no space — rare, e.g., after punctuation)
This means you can reconstruct the original text by simply concatenating all decoded tokens — no need for a separate de-tokenization step with space-insertion rules.

props.question: If a word appears very rarely in the training corpus, what will GPT-2's BPE tokenizer likely do with it?
options: Split it into multiple smaller, more common subword tokens
Leave it as raw bytes without tokenization
props.explanation: BPE never produces unknown tokens — that's one of its key advantages over older tokenizers. Rare words get decomposed into common subword pieces. Even a completely novel word will be split into known byte sequences, since the 256 individual bytes are always in the vocabulary as a fallback. GPT-2 can tokenize literally any byte sequence.

children.children: Beyond the tokens that represent actual text, GPT-2's vocabulary includes one crucial special token.
GPT-2 is minimalist about special tokens. Unlike BERT (which uses [CLS], [SEP], [MASK], [PAD], and more), GPT-2 has essentially one:

rows: Marks the boundary between documents. During training, all of WebText was concatenated into one long stream with this token between documents. It serves as both beginning-of-sequence and end-of-sequence marker.

children.children: That's it. Token 50,256 — the very last entry in the vocabulary, index 50,256 of a 50,257-element table (0-indexed). When GPT-2 generates this token, it's signaling "I'm done." When it sees this token in its input, it knows a new document is starting.

GPT-2 has no padding token (it doesn't do batched variable-length inference in the way BERT does), no separator token (it processes single sequences, not pairs), and no mask token (it's not a masked language model). This simplicity is part of its elegance.

If the text tokens are the dialogue in a play, <|endoftext|> is the stage direction that says "END OF SCENE." It's not part of the story — it's metadata about the story's structure. But it has its own learned embedding, just like any other token, so the model has learned what it means to encounter a scene boundary.

GPT-2 was designed as a pure autoregressive language model: predict the next token, given all previous tokens. This task requires only one structural signal — where documents begin and end. BERT, by contrast, was designed for different tasks (masked prediction, sentence pair classification) that required richer structural markup.

Later GPT variants and chat models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, ChatGPT) added more special tokens for system prompts, user/assistant turn boundaries, function calls, etc. But GPT-2, in its original form, kept things beautifully simple.

props.title: 5. The Vocabulary: A Fixed Universe of Meaning

children.children: Let's step back and appreciate what the tokenizer has produced. We have a vocabulary — a fixed, finite lookup table that maps between tokens (text fragments) and IDs (integers). GPT-2's vocabulary looks something like this:

ID 0     → "!"              (single byte)
ID 1     → "\""             (single byte)
ID 2     → "#"              (single byte)
...
ID 255   → "ÿ"              (last single byte)
ID 256   → first BPE merge  (most common pair)
...
ID 262   → "Ġthe"           (" the" — extremely common)
ID 464   → "The"            (common at sentence starts)
...
ID 3797  → "Ġcat"           (" cat")
...
ID 50256 → "<|endoftext|>"  (the one special token)

Total: 50,257 entries
  - 256 byte tokens (the base alphabet)
  - 50,000 BPE merges (learned subwords)
  - 1 special token (<|endoftext|>)

This vocabulary is GPT-2's entire alphabet. Every piece of text it will ever process must be expressible as a sequence of these 50,257 tokens. Every piece of text it will ever generate must be assembled from these same tokens. It's a closed universe.

The vocabulary was determined entirely during tokenizer training on WebText, before GPT-2's neural network was trained. Once set, it never changes. Because WebText was overwhelmingly English, GPT-2's vocabulary is heavily biased toward English subwords. Chinese characters, Arabic script, or emoji often get split into multiple byte-level tokens, making GPT-2 much less efficient at processing non-English text. A sentence that takes 10 tokens in English might take 30+ tokens in Chinese — eating into that precious 1,024-token context window.

With our text now converted to a sequence of integer IDs, the first act is complete. We've gone from squiggles on a screen to a sequence of numbers. But numbers alone aren't enough for a neural network. We need geometry.

props.title: Act II: From Token IDs to Vectors
6. The Token Embedding Matrix: The Great Lookup Table

children.children: Here is one of the most important and most misunderstood operations in all of deep learning. It is also one of the simplest.
GPT-2 has a matrix called wte ("word token embeddings") — a giant table of numbers. Its dimensions are 50,257 × 768:

wte: GPT-2's token embedding matrix

  V = 50,257  (vocabulary size — one row per token)
  d = 768     (hidden dimension — the size of each embedding vector)

  Shape: 50,257 × 768
  Total parameters: 38,597,376  (about 38.6 million numbers)
  Memory: ~147 MB at float32
Each row of this matrix corresponds to one token in the vocabulary. Row 262 is the embedding for " the". Row 3797 is the embedding for " cat". Row 50256 is the embedding for "<|endoftext|>".
When GPT-2 receives a token ID, it doesn't compute anything. It just looks up the corresponding row. That row — a vector of 768 numbers — IS the token's representation.

# In GPT-2's actual code (simplified from HuggingFace):
# self.wte = nn.Embedding(50257, 768)

token_id = 262            # " the"
embedding = wte[token_id] # Look up row 262 of the 50,257 × 768 matrix
# embedding is now a vector of 768 numbers
# e.g., [0.0423, -0.1891, 0.0712, ..., -0.0334]

The embedding matrix is like a dictionary, but instead of mapping words to definitions (other words), it maps tokens to locations in a vast geometric space. Each token gets assigned a specific point in 768-dimensional space. "Looking up" a token means reading its coordinates.

The embedding lookup is NOT a neural network computation. There's no activation function, no matrix multiplication, no nonlinearity. It's a pure table lookup — index into an array, retrieve a row. The magic is in what those rows contain, which was determined during training on WebText.
Mathematically, the embedding lookup can be expressed as a matrix multiplication with a one-hot vector:

One-hot vector for token 262 (out of 50,257):
[0, 0, 0, ..., 0, 1, 0, ..., 0]  (1 in position 262, 0 everywhere else)
         ↓
Multiply by wte (50,257 × 768):
         ↓
Result: Row 262 of wte (a 768-dim vector)

This is mathematically equivalent to a table lookup — the one-hot vector "selects" a single row. In practice, PyTorch uses actual array indexing (wte.weight[262]) because it's much faster than a 50,257-wide matrix multiplication. But the one-hot perspective is useful theoretically because it shows that embedding lookup is a special case of a linear transformation.

An interesting architectural detail: GPT-2 reuses the token embedding matrix wte at the output. After all 12 transformer layers, the final hidden state is multiplied by the transpose of wte to produce logits over the vocabulary. This is called "weight tying" or "tied embeddings."

This means the same 38.6 million parameters serve double duty — as input embeddings and as the output projection. It saves parameters and creates a nice symmetry: tokens whose embeddings are similar will also have similar output probabilities in similar contexts.

GPT-2's embedding matrix was initialized randomly before training — each entry drawn from a normal distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation 0.02. During training, as the model learned to predict next tokens across the ~8 million WebText documents, these vectors were gradually adjusted by backpropagation. The result is that tokens which appear in similar contexts ended up with similar vectors.

This is the distributional hypothesis made geometric: "You shall know a word by the company it keeps" (J.R. Firth, 1957). Tokens that substitute for each other in sentences — that appear in the same slots, surrounded by the same neighbors — converge to nearby points in GPT-2's 768-dimensional embedding space.
After training, if we measure the cosine similarity between GPT-2's token embeddings, we find patterns like:

similarity(" cat", " dog")     ≈ high   (both common pets, similar contexts)
similarity(" cat", " table")   ≈ low    (different categories)
similarity(" run", " running") ≈ moderate (related but different forms)
similarity(" Paris", " France")≈ moderate (capital-country relationship)

Note: in GPT-2, token embeddings are less individually meaningful than in models trained specifically for embeddings (like Word2Vec). GPT-2's embeddings are just the starting point — the model relies heavily on its 12 transformer layers to build rich contextual representations. The embeddings just need to be "good enough" initial representations.

Critical limitation: at this stage, every occurrence of a token gets the SAME vector, regardless of context. The word "bank" in "river bank" and "bank account" maps to the exact same 768-dimensional point. The embedding doesn't know which meaning is intended. That disambiguation is the job of GPT-2's 12 transformer layers — the attention mechanism will create different contextual representations. But right now, we're still in the preprocessing pipeline, and context hasn't entered the picture yet.
768 dimensions is a lot. We can't visualize it. But we can reason about it, and the geometry turns out to be remarkably structured.
Directions Encode Meaning
The most famous finding about word embeddings is that directions in the space encode semantic relationships. The canonical example:

king - man + woman ≈ queen

In vector terms:
vec("king") - vec("man") + vec("woman") ≈ vec("queen")

What this means: the vector from "man" to "king" (the "royalty" direction) is approximately the same as the vector from "woman" to "queen". The space has organized itself so that abstract relationships like gender, tense, and plurality correspond to consistent directions.

Paris - France + Italy     ≈ Rome       (capital-of direction)
walking - walk + swim      ≈ swimming   (progressive tense direction)
bigger - big + small       ≈ smaller    (comparative direction)

These analogies work because training carved out consistent geometric patterns. GPT-2 never explicitly learned "Paris is to France as Rome is to Italy" — it emerged from the statistics of next-token prediction on WebText.
The king-queen example is famous, but it's also somewhat cherry-picked. In practice:
Many analogies don't work as cleanly. The vector arithmetic gives the right answer maybe 40-70% of the time on analogy benchmarks.
The "directions" aren't perfectly consistent — the gender direction for king/queen isn't identical to the one for actor/actress.
High-frequency words tend to cluster in a particular region of the space, creating a frequency bias.

These examples come from Word2Vec and GloVe — purpose-built embedding models. GPT-2's token embeddings are less individually polished because the model can compensate in later layers. The initial embeddings just need to be a reasonable starting point.
Still, the basic insight holds: the embedding space is geometrically structured, and that structure reflects statistical patterns in language use.

Imagine a city where every building's address encodes information about the building. Buildings on the same street share a function (all restaurants, all hospitals). Buildings at the same numbered cross-street share a price range. The city wasn't planned this way — it emerged from people choosing locations based on their neighbors. That's embedding space: a self-organized city of meaning, where proximity and direction encode relationships that nobody explicitly programmed.

props.question: At the embedding lookup stage (before any transformer layers), what happens when GPT-2 encounters the word 'bank' in two different sentences: 'river bank' and 'bank account'?
options: GPT-2 creates different embeddings based on context
GPT-2 looks up the exact same vector both times
GPT-2 averages the vectors of surrounding words
props.explanation: The token embedding matrix wte is a static lookup table. Token ID → vector. Same ID, same vector, every time. Context-dependent disambiguation happens later, in GPT-2's 12 transformer layers. At this preprocessing stage, 'bank' always maps to the same point in 768-dimensional space.

props.title: Act III: Where Am I? (Positional Encoding)

children.children: We now have a sequence of vectors — one per token. But there's a devastating problem. Consider these two sentences:

Sentence A: "dog bites man"
Sentence B: "man bites dog"

children: Both contain the same three content tokens: "dog", "bites", "man". If we just look up their embeddings from wte, we get the same set of vectors — the same three rows from the same table. But self-attention
, the core operation of a transformer, is permutation-invariant. That means if we feed in the same set of vectors in any order, we get the same output.

children.children: Self-attention is the mechanism where each token looks at every other token in the sequence to decide how much to "pay attention" to it — but crucially, it only cares about *what* the tokens are, not *where* they sit. That's the problem this passage is setting up: since self-attention treats its inputs like a bag of vectors rather than an ordered sequence, "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" would produce identical results without positional encoding to break the symmetry.

Without positional information, "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" are literally indistinguishable to GPT-2. The attention mechanism computes similarity between all pairs of tokens — it doesn't care about order. This is by design (it's what makes attention so powerful and parallelizable), but it means we need to inject position information separately.

Imagine reading a novel where someone has cut out every sentence and tossed them into a bag. You have all the same content, but the story is gone. That's what a transformer without positional encoding faces — a bag of words with no sequence. Position information is what turns the bag back into a narrative.

The solution: add position information directly to the vectors. Stamp each token's embedding with a signal that says "I am at position 0" or "I am at position 42". GPT-2's approach to this is characteristically direct.

GPT-2 solves the position problem in the simplest possible way: it has a second embedding matrix — wpe ("word position embeddings") — and it just looks up the position, exactly like it looks up the token.

wpe: GPT-2's position embedding matrix

  max_seq_length = 1,024  (one row per possible position)
  d = 768                 (same dimension as token embeddings)

  Shape: 1,024 × 768
  Total parameters: 786,432  (about 0.8 million numbers)
  Memory: ~3 MB at float32

That's it. No sines, no cosines, no clever mathematical construction. Just another lookup table with 1,024 rows. Row 0 is the positional embedding for "first token in the sequence." Row 1 is for "second token." Row 1023 is for "1024th and last possible token."

# In GPT-2's actual code (simplified):
# self.wpe = nn.Embedding(1024, 768)

# For a token at position 5:
pos_embedding = wpe[5]  # Look up row 5 of the 1,024 × 768 matrix

# Exactly the same operation as token embedding lookup.
# No computation. Just indexing.

These 786,432 parameters were initialized randomly (just like the token embeddings) and learned during training. The model figured out what positional information it needed and encoded it in these vectors. Nobody told it how to represent position — it discovered a useful scheme on its own.

GPT-2's context window of 1,024 tokens is hard-coded by the size of the position embedding table. There are exactly 1,024 rows in wpe. If you try to input a longer sequence, there's simply no position embedding for position 1,024. This isn't a soft limit — it's a hard architectural constraint. The model literally doesn't have the parameters to represent positions beyond 1,023.

props.title: What Did GPT-2 Learn About Position?

children.children: If we examine the learned position embeddings after training, we find interesting structure:
Nearby positions have similar embeddings — the vector for position 100 is more similar to position 101 than to position 500. The model learned that adjacency matters.
If you project the 1,024 position embeddings into 2D (via PCA or t-SNE), they trace out a roughly smooth curve — the positions are ordered in embedding space.
Early positions (0-50) have somewhat distinct embeddings, likely because the beginnings of documents have special statistical properties (titles, headers, opening sentences).

Some dimensions of the position embeddings appear to encode something like sinusoidal patterns — the model partially rediscovered the original Transformer's approach, because it's a useful inductive bias.

props.title: The Two Lookup Tables Side by Side

children.children: For our input "The cat sat on the mat", GPT-2 performs two parallel lookups:
Two tables, two lookups per token. One says WHAT the token is. The other says WHERE it is. Both are 768-dimensional vectors. Both were learned during training.

props.title: 11. A Historical Note: Other Approaches to Position

children.children: GPT-2's learned positional embeddings are just one solution to the position problem. Understanding the alternatives helps appreciate the design space and why later models moved on.
The original "Attention Is All You Need" paper (Vaswani et al., 2017) used a parameter-free approach: sine and cosine functions at different frequencies.

PE(pos, 2i)   = sin(pos / 10000^(2i/d))
PE(pos, 2i+1) = cos(pos / 10000^(2i/d))

The intuition: each dimension oscillates at a different frequency, like the hands of a clock. The combination of all oscillations creates a unique "timestamp" for each position. The mathematical advantage: the encoding at position (pos + k) can be expressed as a linear function of the encoding at position pos, making it easy for the model to attend to relative positions.
GPT-2's authors chose learned embeddings instead, likely because they found they worked just as well in practice and require no design choices about frequency scales.

Rotary Position Embeddings (Su et al., 2021) take a fundamentally different approach: instead of adding positional vectors to the embeddings, they rotate the query and key vectors inside the attention mechanism by an angle that depends on position.

The key advantage: RoPE naturally encodes relative position (the attention score between tokens depends on their distance, not their absolute positions) and can handle sequence lengths longer than those seen during training. This is why models like Llama, Mistral, and most modern open-source LLMs use RoPE instead of learned absolute positions.
But that's a story for another tutorial. GPT-2 predates RoPE by two years.

props.question: Why does GPT-2 have a hard limit of 1,024 tokens per input?
options: The attention mechanism can't handle more than 1,024 tokens
The position embedding table (wpe) has exactly 1,024 rows — there's no embedding for position 1,024 or beyond
1,024 tokens is the maximum that fits in GPU memory
The tokenizer can only produce 1,024 tokens
props.explanation: GPT-2's context window is hard-coded by the size of its position embedding table wpe, which has shape 1,024 × 768. Positions 0 through 1,023 each have a learned embedding vector; position 1,024 simply has no entry in the table. The tokenizer can produce sequences of any length, and attention could in principle handle them, but without a position embedding, the model can't process them.

props.title: Act IV: The Vector That Enters Layer 1

children.children: We've arrived at the moment of synthesis. For each token in our input sequence, GPT-2 has looked up two vectors from two tables. Now it combines them with the simplest possible operation: addition.

# GPT-2's actual input computation (from HuggingFace, simplified):
# This is the ENTIRE preprocessing pipeline.

def forward(self, input_ids):
    # input_ids: [464, 3797, 3332, 319, 262, 2291]  (our 6 tokens)
    
    position_ids = torch.arange(len(input_ids))  # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
    
    token_embeds = self.wte(input_ids)      # shape: (6, 768)
    pos_embeds   = self.wpe(position_ids)    # shape: (6, 768)
    
    hidden_states = token_embeds + pos_embeds  # shape: (6, 768)
    
    # That's it. hidden_states goes directly into Layer 1.
    # No layer norm. No dropout (at inference). Just addition.
For our example:

x_0 = wte[464]  + wpe[0]   # "The" at position 0  → 768-dim vector
x_1 = wte[3797] + wpe[1]   # " cat" at position 1 → 768-dim vector
x_2 = wte[3332] + wpe[2]   # " sat" at position 2 → 768-dim vector
x_3 = wte[319]  + wpe[3]   # " on" at position 3  → 768-dim vector
x_4 = wte[262]  + wpe[4]   # " the" at position 4 → 768-dim vector
x_5 = wte[2291] + wpe[5]   # " mat" at position 5 → 768-dim vector

Each of these vectors is 768-dimensional. It carries information about both what the token is and where it is. These six vectors, stacked into a matrix of shape (6 × 768), are the input to GPT-2's first transformer block.

If the token embedding is a person's identity — their name, personality, skills — then the positional embedding is their assigned seat in a theater. The combined vector says: "I am 'cat,' and I am sitting in seat 1." Both pieces of information matter. Who you are determines how you interact with others. Where you sit determines who you can easily interact with.
An obvious alternative would be to concatenate the two vectors — put the 768 token dimensions next to the 768 position dimensions to get a 1,536-dimensional vector. Why does GPT-2 add instead?

Addition keeps the dimension at 768, meaning all downstream layers (attention, feed-forward) work with 768-dimensional vectors. Concatenation would double the size, doubling the cost of every subsequent computation.
Addition forces the model to share the 768 dimensions between token and position information. This creates a form of information compression that, empirically, works well.
The first layer's attention can immediately compute token-position interactions without needing to learn cross-dimensional correlations.

Is information lost by jamming two signals into one vector via addition? In theory, yes — but in practice, 768 dimensions is a lot of room. The model learns to use some dimensions primarily for token identity, others primarily for position, and some for both.

props.title: 13. No Layer Norm Here (GPT-2's Post-Norm Architecture)

children.children: You might expect some kind of normalization step before the vectors enter the first transformer block. Many tutorials mention "layer norm" as part of the preprocessing. But GPT-2 does NOT apply layer normalization at this point.

GPT-2 uses what's called a post-norm (or post-LN) architecture. The layer normalization happens after the attention and feed-forward sub-layers, not before them. The structure of each GPT-2 block looks like:

GPT-2 Block structure (post-norm):

  Input x
    ↓
  Multi-Head Attention
    ↓
  Add residual (x + attention_output)
    ↓
  Layer Norm        ← normalization happens AFTER attention
    ↓
  Feed-Forward Network
    ↓
  Add residual
    ↓
  Layer Norm        ← normalization happens AFTER FFN
    ↓
  Output

This means the raw sum of token embeddings and position embeddings — with no normalization — goes directly into the first block's multi-head attention. The vectors arrive at Layer 1 in whatever scale and distribution the learned embeddings happen to have.

This is a key difference from many later models. GPT-3, Llama, Mistral, and most modern transformers use pre-norm (also called pre-LN) architecture, where layer norm is applied BEFORE attention and FFN. Pre-norm makes training more stable and enables deeper models. But GPT-2 predates this convention. If you're reading about transformers and see "layer norm is applied before the first layer," that's true for newer models but not for GPT-2.
GPT-2 does have a final layer norm (ln_f) after the last transformer block, before the output projection. But between the embedding addition and Layer 1's attention? Nothing. The vectors go in raw.
Let's take stock. Starting from "The cat sat on the mat", we now have:

Input matrix X:  shape (6 × 768)

  Row 0: [0.052, -0.186, 0.073, ...]  ← wte[464] + wpe[0]   = "The" at pos 0
  Row 1: [0.141, -0.052, 0.094, ...]  ← wte[3797] + wpe[1]  = " cat" at pos 1
  Row 2: [0.089, -0.117, 0.045, ...]  ← wte[3332] + wpe[2]  = " sat" at pos 2
  Row 3: [0.035, -0.221, 0.012, ...]  ← wte[319] + wpe[3]   = " on" at pos 3
  Row 4: [0.068, -0.143, 0.081, ...]  ← wte[262] + wpe[4]   = " the" at pos 4
  Row 5: [0.097, -0.089, 0.056, ...]  ← wte[2291] + wpe[5]  = " mat" at pos 5

Each row = token_embedding + position_embedding
No layer norm. No other processing. Just two lookups and an addition.

This matrix is the starting state of what's called the residual stream. In the residual stream framework (popularized by Elhage et al., 2021), each transformer layer reads from this stream, computes something, and writes the result back by addition. Layer after layer, the representations get richer and more contextual.

But that's the future. Right now, at the entrance to Layer 1, this matrix is the entire state of the computation. Every piece of information GPT-2 has access to is encoded in these 6 × 768 = 4,608 numbers.
Let's count every parameter involved in getting from text to the input of Layer 1:

Component                  Shape           Parameters
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Token embeddings (wte)     50,257 × 768    38,597,376
Position embeddings (wpe)  1,024 × 768        786,432
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Total preprocessing params                 39,383,808

GPT-2 small total params:                124,439,808

Preprocessing is 31.6% of all parameters!

Nearly a third of GPT-2's parameters are in the embedding tables. This is typical of smaller language models — the vocabulary embedding is a fixed cost regardless of model depth. In GPT-2 medium (345M params) and large (774M params), the embedding fraction shrinks because the transformer layers get bigger while the vocabulary stays the same.

The input to Layer 1 is a matrix of shape (sequence_length × 768). Each row is one token's representation, containing both its identity (from wte) and its position (from wpe), combined by addition. This is the residual stream's initial state — the starting point from which all of GPT-2's 'understanding' will be built through 12 layers of attention and feed-forward processing.

As we stand at the threshold of Layer 1, it's worth reflecting on what our input representation does NOT contain. This is just as important as what it does contain, because it defines exactly what GPT-2's 12 transformer layers must accomplish.
No contextual information.

children: The vector for "bank" is the same whether the surrounding words are "river" or "account". Each token's vector was looked up independently from wte — no interaction between tokens has occurred.

children.children: No syntactic structure.

children: GPT-2 doesn't know that "cat" is the subject and "mat" is part of a prepositional phrase. Syntax must be discovered by the attention layers.

children.children: No coreference.

children: If the text said "The cat sat on the mat. It was soft.", GPT-2 doesn't yet know that "it" refers to "mat" (or possibly "cat"). That linking happens in the layers.

children.children: No world knowledge activation.

children: The embedding for "cat" might be near "dog" and "pet" in the space, but GPT-2 hasn't yet activated its stored knowledge that cats are small, furry, independent, etc. That knowledge is stored in the feed-forward layers' weight matrices and gets activated during the forward pass.

children.children: No prediction.

children: GPT-2 hasn't even begun to think about what comes next. Its entire job — predicting the next token — is performed by the 12 transformer blocks downstream.

children.children: And yet — and this is the remarkable part — everything GPT-2 needs to build all of that is present in these initial vectors. The token embeddings encode rough semantic similarity. The positional embeddings encode sequence order. From these two ingredients, through 12 layers of attention and feed-forward transformations, GPT-2 will construct representations rich enough to generate coherent, contextually appropriate text.

We're at the moment just after the Big Bang, when the universe consisted of a hot, uniform plasma. No stars, no galaxies, no planets, no life. But all the matter and energy were there. All the physical laws were in place. Everything that followed — the cooling, the condensation, the formation of structure — was a consequence of those initial conditions. The vector that enters Layer 1 is GPT-2's primordial plasma: undifferentiated, but pregnant with possibility.
Let's trace the complete journey one final time, with all of GPT-2's specific numbers:
A string of characters arrives: "The cat sat on the mat"
GPT-2's byte-level BPE tokenizer (trained on WebText) segments it: ["The", " cat", " sat", " on", " the", " mat"]
Each token is mapped to an integer ID from the 50,257-token vocabulary: [464, 3797, 3332, 319, 262, 2291]
Each token ID is used to look up a 768-dimensional vector from wte (the 50,257 × 768 token embedding matrix)
Each position index (0-5) is used to look up a 768-dimensional vector from wpe (the 1,024 × 768 position embedding matrix)
The token and position vectors are added together: input = wte[token_id] + wpe[position]
The result — a matrix of shape (6 × 768) — enters Block 1 directly, with no layer norm

The entire preprocessing pipeline involves exactly two embedding tables (39.4 million parameters), two lookups, and one addition. No neural network computation. No activation functions. No attention. Nothing nonlinear.

And yet in perhaps a microsecond of compute time, we've gone from squiggles to geometry. From characters to meaning-bearing vectors in a 768-dimensional space, tagged with their positions in the sequence. Like Weinberg's first three minutes of the universe, these early moments are deterministic, quiet, and often overlooked. But they set the initial conditions that determine everything that follows.
"The effort to understand the universe is one of the very few things which lifts human life a little above the level of farce and gives it some of the grace of tragedy." — Steven Weinberg
The effort to understand how a transformer turns text into meaning begins here, in these quiet first moments before the layers begin their work.

props.question: Which of the following correctly describes GPT-2's complete preprocessing pipeline (everything before Layer 1)?
options: Tokenization → Embedding lookup → Sinusoidal positional encoding → Layer norm
Tokenization → Embedding lookup (wte) → Position lookup (wpe) → Addition → Layer norm → Layer 1
Tokenization → Embedding lookup (wte) → Position lookup (wpe) → Addition → Layer 1 (no layer norm)
Tokenization → One-hot encoding → Matrix multiplication → RoPE rotation → Layer 1
props.explanation: GPT-2's preprocessing is: (1) BPE tokenization to get token IDs, (2) look up token embeddings from wte (50,257 × 768), (3) look up position embeddings from wpe (1,024 × 768), (4) add them together. The resulting vectors go directly into Layer 1 — GPT-2 uses post-norm architecture, so there's no layer norm before the first attention computation. No sinusoidal encoding (that was the original Transformer). No RoPE (that came later, in 2021).
